does: This paper introduces MM-JudgeBench, a large-scale multilingual and multimodal benchmark containing over 60K pairwise preference instances across 25 typologically diverse languages for evaluating vision-language model (LVLM) judges and reward models. The benchmark combines two complementary subsets: a general vision-language preference evaluation extending VL-RewardBench and a chart-centric visual-text reasoning subset from OpenCQA. The authors conduct systematic evaluation of 22 LVLMs (15 open-source and 7 proprietary) to assess how well these reward models generalize across languages, revealing substantial cross-linguistic performance variations and limitations in current evaluation approaches.
connects: This work extends recent research on automatic evaluation of vision-language models, building on established frameworks like VL-RewardBench and methodologies for assessing reward models in alignment. It responds to the widespread limitation in the field that evaluation benchmarks remain almost exclusively English-centric, despite growing global deployment of LVLMs. The paper connects to broader multilingual NLP evaluation efforts and visual reasoning benchmarks, while addressing a critical gap where cross-lingual robustness of vision-language judges has been largely unexamined. It leverages existing datasets like OpenCQA and contributes a new multilingual training set (MM-RewardBench) for domain adaptation, advancing the conversation around generalizable evaluation metrics.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into building more robust, multilingual reward models and judges that work reliably across diverse linguistic and cultural contexts. It supports domain adaptation approaches for generalizing vision-language evaluators through the provided multilingual training data. The systematic analysis of cross-linguistic performance gaps motivates development of better alignment and evaluation techniques that account for language-specific challenges. This work establishes infrastructure for studying how vision-language understanding degrades across typologically diverse languages, potentially spurring investigation into multilingual pretraining strategies, language-aware evaluation metrics, and techniques for improving cultural and linguistic robustness of LVLM-based systems at scale.
